title: Beyond Structure: Revolutionising Materials Discovery via AI-Driven Synthesis Protocol-Property Relationships
abstract: The current structure-centric paradigm in artificial intelligence (AI)-driven materials discovery, despite delivering thousands of candidate structures, is stalling at a critical barrier: the synthesizability gap. We argue that closing this gap demands a pivot to a synthesis-first paradigm in which executable synthesis protocols, not just atomic configurations, are treated as primary design variables. We outline a roadmap built on three pillars: (i) representing synthesis procedures as machine-readable protocols, (ii) deploying generative and inverse-design models to propose actionable reaction pathways and recipes, and (iii) integrating closed-loop optimisation to refine protocols against experimental realities and sustainability constraints. Framed in terms of the causal backbone P->X->y from protocol P to structure X and properties y, this perspective sets out methodological building blocks, standards needs and self-driving laboratory (SDL) integration strategies to accelerate reproducible, data-first materials discovery.
summary: The paper argues that AI-driven materials discovery must move beyond predicting stable structures to predicting the actual synthesis recipes required to make them. By treating synthesis protocols as primary design variables, the author proposes a way to close the gap between computational predictions and experimental reality using autonomous laboratories.
main_problem: The 'synthesizability gap' in materials discovery, where AI models predict stable atomic structures but fail to provide actionable, experimentally feasible synthesis protocols.
main_result: The paper proposes a paradigm shift from a structure-centric (Structure -> Property) to a synthesis-first (Protocol -> Structure -> Property) framework to bridge the gap between virtual candidates and laboratory realization.
method: A roadmap for integrating machine-readable synthesis protocols, generative/inverse-design models (VAEs, GANs, Diffusion), and closed-loop optimization via Self-Driving Laboratories (SDLs).
model_or_system: The framework focuses on inorganic and solid-state materials (e.g., semiconductors, batteries, MOFs) and models the causal chain P -> X -> y, where P is the synthesis protocol, X is the resulting structure/phase, and y is the final property.
key_observables: Intermediate structural phases, morphology, defects, and final material properties like ionic conductivity or catalytic activity.
important_parameters: Synthesis parameters (temperature, pressure, pH, time, precursors) and sustainability metrics (E-factor, energy footprint).
main_assumptions: The effectiveness of the paradigm shift relies on the availability of standardized, machine-readable protocols and the ability to scale high-quality, multi-modal datasets.
figures_summary: Figure 1 compares the conventional structure-centric workflow with the proposed protocol-centric workflow that closes the synthesizability gap. Figure 2 illustrates an integrated ecosystem linking data sources, AI cores, and automated execution.
paper_structure: The paper identifies the synthesizability gap, proposes a new P->X->y framework, outlines methodological pillars (representation, generative modeling, and closed-loop integration), provides case studies across material domains, and concludes with future challenges and a vision for 2035.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Paradigm shift from structure-centric to synthesis protocol-centric discovery. (A) Conventional workflow emphasises virtual structure generation and property prediction, leaving a dashed synthesizability gap to experimental realisation. (B) The proposed workflow elevates executable synthesis protocols to first- class design objects and closes the loop via autonomous experimentation.

Figure 2: Integrated ecosystem linking heterogeneous data sources, protocol representations, AI/ML core and automated execution/characterisation in a closed-loop discovery cycle. Characterisation streams feed both P →X and X →y models, enabling time-resolved updating during closed-loop operation.
